titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Nathan Whiting Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Betsy H. Morris and Staff of Manuscripts and Archives

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Saturday March 21, 2020 at 07:56
Finding aid revision description not supplied.

did.unittitle: Nathan Whiting papers
did.abstract: The papers consist of family correspondence of the Whiting family as well as the personal correspondence of Nathan Whiting, military and legal documents and miscellaneous family papers, including an address by Polly Whiting on the importance of education for women (undated), an essay by Samuel Whiting on his marriage (pre-1725) and a plan of the Township of Cumberland showing land belonging to Colonel Nathan Whiting and others..

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Nathan Whiting Papers (MS 552). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Gift of Minnesota Historical Society, 2006 and 2011.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: The entire collection, with the exception of Accession 2006-M-099 and 2012-M-007, is available on microfilm (389 frames on 1 reel, 35mm.) from Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library, at cost. Order no. HM 110.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in four parts and two additions: I. Correspondence. II. Wrtitings and Legal Papers. III. Miscellaneous and Unidentified. IV. Oversize.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Nathan Whiting, the youngest of thirteen children of the Rev. Samuel and Elizabeth (Adams) Whiting of Windham, Connecticut, was born in that town, May 4, 1724, being a brother of Colonel. John Whiting (Y. C 1726) His father died when this son was 16 months old, and when he was in his 14th Year, his mother married the Rev. Samuel Niles (Harv. 1699), of Braintree, Massachusetts. Nathan Whiting was mainly brought up by his sister Mary, and her husband, Rector Clap.

He studied at the College for two years after graduation, and then settled in New Haven as a merchant, but accompanied as Ensign the Connecticut troops in the expedition against Louisburg in 1745; in this service he so distinguished himself, that at the end of the campaign, a lieutenancy in the British army was given him by Sir William Pepperrell. A little later he was in business-partnership with Thomas Darling (Y. C 1740). While in this employment the French war began, in 1755; and he was appointed, in March of that year, Lieutenant Colonel of the 2d Connecticut Regiment. He assisted in garrisoning Fort Edward; and on September 8, was with Colonel Ephraim Williams in the detachment sent against Baron Dieskau. On the fall of Williams, the command devolved on Lieutenant Colonel Whiting, who conducted the retreat of the corps with a skill and coolness which did him high credit. When the next expedition was sent out in 1756, he was promoted to a Colonelcy. He continued in the service throughout the war, and was esteemed by both British and Americans an officer of uncommon merit.
In 1769 and 1770 he was a representative of New Haven in the General Assembly; and at the time of his death he was in nomination for the Upper House.
He died in New Haven, April 9, 1771, at the age of 47. His estate was inventoried at £1322.

He married, July 12, 1750, Mary, daughter of Captain Rosewell and Mary (Haynes) Saltonstall, of Branford, her mother having married Rector Clap in 1741. Of their five sons and four daughters, three sons and one daughter survived infancy; the eldest of these children was graduated here in 1777. The widow of Colonel Whiting next married the Rev. Warham Williams (Y. C. 1745), of Northford Parish, in North Branford, Connecticut, whom she survived.

President Dwight thus describes Colonel Whiting: "He was an exemplary professor of the Christian religion; and for refined and dignified manners, and nobleness of mind, has rarely been excelled." His portrait is in the rooms of the Connecticut Historical Society, at Hartford.

Conn. Journal , Apr. 19, 1771 Dwight, "Statistical Account of New Haven", 75. Goodwin, Genealogical Notes , 333, 336-337. Kingsley, Hist. Discourse at New Haven , 68. Larned, Hist of Windham County , i, 564. N. H. Col. Hist. Soc. Papers , iii, 608. Pease and Niles, Gazetteer of Conn. and R. I ., 206.
Franklin B. Dexter, Biographical Sketches of the Graduates of Yale College, 1701-1745 (New York: Henry Holt and Company, 1885), pp. 750-751.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers consist of family correspondence of the Whiting family as well as the personal correspondence of Nathan Whiting, military and legal documents and miscellaneous family papers, including an address by Polly Whiting on the importance of education for women (undated),an essay by Samuel Whiting on his marriage (pre-1725) and a plan of the Township of Cumberland showing land belonging to Colonel Nathan Whiting and others.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.
The entire collection, with the exception of Accession 2006-M-099 and 2012-M-007, is available on microfilm. Patrons must use FILM HM 110 instead of the originals.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection are in the public domain. There are no restrictions on use. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

geogname.part: United States -- History -- Colonial period, ca. 1600-1775
United States -- History -- French and Indian War, 1754-1763

did.unittitle: Inventory

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Patrons must use microfilm (FILM HM 110) instead of the originals.

did.unittitle: Correspondence
Amherst, Jeffrey, 1st baron Amherst, to Nathan Whiting (typescript)
Brattle, (Mrs.) K., to Mary (Haynes) Saltonstall Clap
Clap, Mary (Haynes) Saltonstall), to Nathan Whiting
Eliot, Jared, to Nathan Whiting
Hale, Elisha, to Mary (Saltonstall) Whiting Williams
Hale, George, to Elisha Hale
Hale, George, to an unidentified brother
Hale, Thomas, to an unidentified brother
Irwin, [J.L.?], to Elisha Hale
Jones, Betsey, of Wilkes Barre, Pennsylvania, to her uncle
Saltonstall, Rosewell, to Mary (Haynes) Saltonstall Clap
Whiting, Nathan
to Mary (Haynes) Saltonstall Clap
to James [Fitter?], requesting appointment as collector of the port of New London
to Sir William Johnson (photostat)
to Roswell Saltonstall
to Mary (Saltonstall) Whiting Williams
Whiting, Samuel, to Warham and Mary (Saltonstall) Whiting Williams
Williams, Mary (Saltonstall) Whiting, to Nathan Whiting
Writings and Legal Papers
Hale, Elisha, of Glastonbury, Connecticut: deed to Nathan W. Hale of Glastonbury, Connecticut
Lucas, Samuel, of Middletown, Connecticut: deed to Sarah Haynes of Hartford, Connecticut
Saltonstall, Roswell: deed to his sisters, Mary (Saltonstall) Whiting Williams and Katharine (Saltonstall) Wells
Seymour, Timothy, of Hartford, Connecticut: miscellaneous papers
Whiting Nathan
Obituary for Samuel Whiting (copy of newspaper article)
Military papers re: enrollments and expenditures
Inventory of his estate
Whiting, Nathan, family: vital records, with remarks about mortality
Whiting, Polly: address on the importance of education for women
Clap, Thomas, "Meditations on the Death of My Wife" manuscript

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript, unsigned, by Thomas Clap on the death of his wife. Includes a manuscript annotation in an unidentified hand stating that the manuscript was written by Samuel Whiting. A more complete version of the Clap manuscript is available in Thomas Clap, President of Yale College, Records (RU 130), Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

did.unittitle: Williams, Mary (Saltonstall) Whiting: inventory of her estate
Miscellaneous and Unidentified
"An Elegy of the Death of Miss Elizabeth Saltonstall"
"A Cattelouge of the Books Given to Roswell from his Honrd Father"
Invitation to a "Ball, in Celebration of Peace," in Wethersfield, Connecticut
Oversize
Whiting, Mary: letter to her mother, Mary (Saltonstall) Whiting Williams
Whiting, Nathan: "A [Relation?) of Provision Money due to the Tenth Company of the [2nd?] Conn. Reg. Commanded by N. Whiting Esq, from the Time of Enlistments to the Time of Receiv. King's Provis."
Whiting, Nathan, and Mary (Saltonstall) Whiting Williams: photographs of portraits
Additional Material

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This accession consists of an order book presumably kept by Nathan Whiting while on a march with British and Colonial troops from Lake Ontario in New York to Montreal, Canada, during the French and Indian War. Information is given on routine army matters, camp life, deserters, court martials, inspections, and the surrender of Montreal by the French to the British.

did.unittitle: Order book
Additional Material
Township of Cumberland plat map

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: A copy of this material is available in digital form from Manuscripts and Archives. Contact Manuscripts and Archives at mssa.assist@yale.edu to request access to the digital copy.
